section.title: Manasseh and Amon
section.sectionRationale: Manasseh becomes Judah's worst king, rebuilding the high places his father tore down, setting up altars to the host of heaven in the temple itself, and even sacrificing his own sons in the fire. Yet when Assyria carries him away in bronze shackles, Manasseh humbles himself and earnestly seeks the LORD, and God graciously restores him to his kingdom. His son Amon, however, repeats his father's earlier sins without ever repeating his repentance, and is assassinated within two years.
section.thesis: Manasseh's story, from the deepest recorded evil of any king of Judah to genuine, God honored repentance in the depths of exile, shows that no sin places anyone beyond the reach of God's mercy, while his son Amon's refusal to learn that same lesson shows that a parent's late repentance guarantees nothing for a child who will not humble himself in turn.
section.frameNote: Three beats: Manasseh's unmatched idolatry, verses 1 to 9; his captivity, repentance, and restoration, verses 10 to 17; and the summary of his reign followed by Amon's brief, unrepentant one, verses 18 to 25.

verses.text: Manasseh was twelve years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem fifty-five years.
And he did evil in the sight of the LORD by following the abominations of the nations that the LORD had driven out before the Israelites.
For he rebuilt the high places that his father Hezekiah had torn down, and he raised up altars for the Baals and made Asherah poles. And he worshiped and served all the host of heaven.
Manasseh also built altars in the house of the LORD, of which the LORD had said, "My Name will remain in Jerusalem forever."
In both courtyards of the house of the LORD, he built altars to all the host of heaven.

He sacrificed his sons in the fire in the Valley of Ben-hinnom. He practiced sorcery, {{qesem|divination}}, and witchcraft, and consulted mediums and spiritists. He did great evil in the sight of the LORD, provoking Him to anger.

Manasseh even took the carved image he had made and set it up in the house of God, of which God had said to David and his son Solomon, "In this temple and in Jerusalem, which I have chosen out of all the tribes of Israel, I will establish My Name forever.

I will never again cause the feet of the Israelites to leave the land that I assigned to your fathers, if only they are careful to do all that I have commanded them through Moses, all the laws, statutes, and judgments."
So Manasseh led the people of Judah and Jerusalem astray, so that they did greater evil than the nations that the LORD had destroyed before the Israelites.
And the LORD spoke to Manasseh and his people, but they did not listen.
So the LORD brought against them the military commanders of the king of Assyria, who captured Manasseh, put a hook in his nose, bound him with bronze shackles, and took him to Babylon.
And in his distress, Manasseh sought the {{chanan|favor}} of the LORD his God and earnestly {{kana|humbled}} himself before the God of his fathers.
And when he prayed to Him, the LORD received his plea and heard his petition. So He brought him back to Jerusalem and to his kingdom. Then Manasseh knew that the LORD is God.

After this, Manasseh rebuilt the outer wall of the City of David from west of Gihon in the valley to the entrance of the Fish Gate, and he brought it around the hill of Ophel and heightened it considerably. He also stationed military commanders in all the fortified cities of Judah.
He removed the foreign gods and the idol from the house of the LORD, along with all the altars he had built on the temple mount and in Jerusalem, and he dumped them outside the city.
Then he restored the altar of the LORD and sacrificed peace offerings and thank offerings on it, and he told Judah to serve the LORD, the God of Israel.
Nevertheless, the people still sacrificed at the high places, but only to the LORD their God.

As for the rest of the acts of Manasseh, along with his prayer to his God and the words of the seers who spoke to him in the name of the LORD, the God of Israel, they are indeed written in the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel.

His prayer and how God received his plea, as well as all his sin and unfaithfulness, and the sites where he built high places and set up Asherah poles and idols before he humbled himself, they are indeed written in the Records of the Seers.
And Manasseh rested with his fathers and was buried at his palace. And his son Amon reigned in his place.
Amon was twenty-two years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem two years.
And he did evil in the sight of the LORD, as his father Manasseh had done. Amon served and sacrificed to all the idols that his father Manasseh had made,
but he did not humble himself before the LORD as his father Manasseh had done; instead, Amon increased his guilt.
Then the servants of Amon conspired against him and killed him in his palace.
But the people of the land killed all those who had conspired against King Amon, and they made his son Josiah king in his place.

qesem.gloss: Divination or fortune telling. Manasseh practices qesem alongside sorcery, witchcraft, and consulting mediums, all explicitly forbidden in Deuteronomy 18:10-12; the sheer accumulation of occult practices marks his reign as an almost total embrace of everything the Law names as detestable.

chanan.gloss: To be gracious to, show favor, or seek favor. In captivity Manasseh sought the chanan, favor, of the LORD, the same root behind the word grace; even Judah's worst king appeals not to his own merit but to God's gracious favor, and finds it granted.

kana.gloss: To humble, subdue, or bring low. Manasseh earnestly kana, humbled, himself before the God of his fathers; the same root describes Amon's explicit failure to do the same, verse 23, making Manasseh's humbling the very thing his son refuses to imitate despite having every reason to know its results.

basic.context: Manasseh becomes the worst king in Judah's history, rebuilding every high place his father Hezekiah had destroyed, setting up altars to astral deities in the temple's own courtyards, sacrificing his own sons in the fire, and filling the land with sorcery, divination, and mediums, provoking the LORD to greater anger than even the pagan nations Israel had displaced. When the LORD's warnings go unheeded, Assyria captures Manasseh, puts a hook in his nose, and drags him in bronze shackles to Babylon. There, in the depths of his distress, Manasseh does what no one could have expected: he seeks the LORD's favor and earnestly humbles himself, and God, remarkably, hears his prayer and restores him to his throne in Jerusalem. Manasseh spends his remaining years undoing his own worst work, removing the idols and restoring the LORD's altar, though the high places persist among the people. His son Amon, however, repeats his father's earlier idolatry in full but never repeats his father's repentance, and is assassinated within two years by his own servants.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recording both the depths evil can reach and the height God's mercy can still rise to meet it. It is not suggesting repentance can be delayed indefinitely without cost, since Manasseh's fifty five year reign includes decades of devastating idolatry before his change of heart, but it does establish clearly that no sin, however severe or prolonged, places anyone beyond the reach of God's grace when genuine humbling occurs.
basic.originalLanguages: Manasseh's evil includes qesem, divination, alongside sorcery and mediums, an almost complete catalogue of everything Deuteronomy 18 forbids. Yet in captivity he seeks the LORD's chanan, favor or grace, and earnestly kana, humbles, himself, the very verb his own son Amon is explicitly said never to imitate, verse 23, making the contrast between father and son turn on this single word.
basic.typology: Manasseh's captivity in bronze shackles, followed by his restoration to his own kingdom upon genuine repentance, previews the pattern of exile and return that structures the whole story of God's people, and anticipates the gospel truth that even those most bound by their own sin can be set free and restored the moment they truly turn to God in humility.
basic.christ: Manasseh stands as one of Scripture's most vivid Old Testament pictures of grace reaching the worst of sinners; the very king who filled the temple with idols and shed his own children's blood is, upon genuine repentance, fully restored, a pattern the New Testament echoes explicitly in Christ, who came to save sinners, of whom Paul says he himself was foremost, 1 Timothy 1:15-16, precisely so that in him Christ Jesus might display His perfect patience as an example to those who would believe. No one reading Manasseh's restoration has grounds to believe their own sin has placed them beyond the reach of the same mercy.
basic.god: God's justice is severe against sustained, extreme wickedness, provoking Him to bring devastating consequence on Manasseh through Assyrian captivity. Yet God's mercy proves even more remarkable than His justice; the moment Manasseh genuinely humbles himself, even from the depths of exile, God hears and restores him completely, showing that His grace responds to the humblest cry rather than requiring a spotless record before it can be extended.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 18:10-12 forbids the specific occult practices, sorcery, divination, and consulting mediums, that Manasseh embraces wholesale. 2 Kings 21 gives the parallel account of Manasseh's reign, though without the account of his repentance found only here in Chronicles. 1 Timothy 1:15-16 identifies Christ's mission to save even the foremost of sinners as a pattern and encouragement for all who despair of their own sin. Luke 15:20-24 pictures the father running to meet the returning prodigal, the same undeserved welcome Manasseh receives from God.
basic.oneStory: Manasseh's descent into the deepest recorded evil of any king of Judah, followed by his genuine repentance and full restoration from the very depths of captivity, is one of Scripture's clearest demonstrations that no sin places a person beyond the reach of God's mercy when true humbling occurs. His son Amon's failure to learn that same lesson, repeating the sin without ever repeating the repentance, warns that a parent's late grace is no substitute for a child's own turning to God, a pattern this entire story anticipates being finally and fully resolved only in Christ, who saves sinners as great as Manasseh and calls each new generation to humble itself before Him directly.

context.literary: The chapter is structured around a single stunning reversal: catalogued, escalating evil, verses 1 to 9, met by the LORD's initial unheeded warning, verse 10, then captivity, verse 11, and an unexpected, decisive turn to repentance and restoration, verses 12 to 17, before Amon's brief, unrepeated repentance closes the account in verses 18 to 25 as a sobering contrast.
context.historical: Manasseh's fifty five year reign, the longest of any king of Judah, spans a period of heavy Assyrian dominance in the region; his captivity likely reflects Assyrian policy of summoning or forcibly transporting vassal kings suspected of disloyalty, consistent with extra biblical Assyrian records naming Manasseh among tribute paying vassal kings of this era.
context.canonical: This chapter includes the only biblical record of Manasseh's repentance, absent from the parallel account in 2 Kings 21, which the chronicler likely includes deliberately to demonstrate that even the worst king in Judah's history was not beyond the reach of restoration, a message especially significant for the chronicler's postexilic audience, themselves seeking restoration after their own captivity.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record both the unmatched depths of Manasseh's idolatry, provoking God's anger more than the pagan nations before Israel, and his genuine, God honored repentance from the depths of Assyrian captivity, contrasting this with his son Amon's refusal to learn the same lesson of humility, so that readers see both the reality of severe judgment and the greater reality of God's mercy toward genuine repentance.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates a specific, extreme historical case of both apostasy and repentance; it is not suggesting that severe or prolonged sin is therefore harmless, since real, devastating consequences of Manasseh's evil persist even after his own repentance, evident in the ongoing corrupted practices among the people, verse 17, and in the subsequent Babylonian exile which 2 Kings 21:10-15 explicitly attributes partly to Manasseh's sins. What endures is the principle that genuine repentance, however late, is always met by God's mercy, without erasing every consequence already set in motion.

debates.question: Why does the parallel account in 2 Kings 21 omit any mention of Manasseh's repentance and restoration, recording only his evil?

views.label: Different theological emphases suited to each book's purpose
views.case: Kings, written to explain why the exile happened, emphasizes Manasseh's role in provoking that judgment, 2 Kings 21:10-15, while Chronicles, written to encourage a returned exilic community, emphasizes that restoration after genuine repentance, even after severe sin, is always possible, each book selecting the material that serves its larger purpose.

views.label: Historical material available to the chronicler but not to the author of Kings
views.case: The chronicler explicitly cites separate source material, the words of the seers and the Records of the Seers, verses 18 to 19, suggesting access to additional historical records concerning Manasseh's captivity and prayer that were not incorporated into the earlier composition of Kings.

debates.bearing: Both explanations affirm that Manasseh's repentance is a genuine historical event recorded by the chronicler for readers who especially needed to hear that even the worst of sinners, and the worst of Judah's kings, was not beyond the reach of God's restoring grace.

study.originalLanguages: Manasseh's catalogue of evil includes qesem, divination, alongside sorcery and consulting mediums, nearly every occult practice forbidden by the Law in one reign. Yet in captivity he seeks the LORD's chanan, favor or grace, and kana, humbles, himself earnestly, the identical verb his own son Amon is explicitly said to have refused, making the entire chapter turn on whether one generation's humbling will be imitated by the next.

typology.type: A king bound in shackles and exiled who is restored to his throne upon genuine repentance
typology.fulfillment: The exile and return pattern fulfilled ultimately in Christ's rescue of sinners bound by their own sin and restored to fellowship with God through repentance and faith, Colossians 1:13-14.

typology.type: God hearing and receiving the plea of the worst offender the moment he genuinely turns to Him
typology.fulfillment: Christ's mission to save even the foremost of sinners, displaying His perfect patience as a pattern for all who would believe, 1 Timothy 1:15-16.

study.christ: Manasseh stands as perhaps the Old Testament's most vivid demonstration that no sin, however extreme or prolonged, places a person beyond the reach of God's mercy, since even the king who filled the temple with idols and sacrificed his own sons is, upon genuine humbling, fully forgiven and restored. This anticipates the gospel's central claim about Christ Jesus, who came into the world to save sinners, of whom I am foremost, so that in me, as the foremost of sinners, Christ Jesus might display His perfect patience as an example, 1 Timothy 1:15-16, an example every reader of Manasseh's story is invited to trust for themselves.
study.god: God's justice against sustained, extreme wickedness is severe, allowing Manasseh's own captivity in bronze shackles as direct consequence of his provocations. Yet God's mercy exceeds even that severity; the moment genuine humility appears, however deep the prior sin and however late in life it comes, God hears, receives, and fully restores, revealing a God whose grace is not exhausted even by the worst possible offense against Him.

crossReferences.note: Forbids the specific occult practices, divination, sorcery, and consulting mediums, that Manasseh embraces in near total form.
The parallel account of Manasseh's reign, which records his evil in detail but omits the account of his repentance found only in Chronicles.
Christ Jesus came to save sinners, of whom I am foremost, the New Testament pattern of grace reaching the worst of sinners, paralleling Manasseh's restoration.
The father running to meet the returning prodigal son, the same undeserved, joyful welcome Manasseh receives from God upon his return.

study.oneStory: Manasseh's unmatched descent into idolatry, child sacrifice, and occult practice, followed by his genuine repentance and full restoration from the depths of captivity, stands as one of Scripture's most powerful demonstrations that no sin places a person beyond the reach of God's mercy when true humbling occurs before Him. His son Amon's tragic failure to learn that same lesson warns that grace received by a parent is never automatically inherited by a child who refuses to humble himself in turn, a pattern this whole account anticipates being finally answered in Christ, who saves sinners as great as Manasseh and calls every generation to seek that same mercy directly for itself.

quiz.q: How is the severity of Manasseh's evil described in comparison to the nations Israel had displaced?

options.text: Manasseh led Judah to do greater evil than the nations the LORD had destroyed before the Israelites.
options.note: Verse 9 gives this exact, striking comparison.

options.text: His evil was considerably milder than the surrounding pagan nations.
options.note: The text explicitly states the opposite, that Judah under Manasseh did greater evil than those nations.

options.text: His evil was limited to a single act of building one high place.
options.note: Verses 2 to 9 describe an extensive range of idolatry, child sacrifice, and occult practice, far beyond a single act.

options.text: No comparison to other nations is given.
options.note: Verse 9 explicitly makes this comparison.

quiz.q: What happened to Manasseh as a direct consequence of his and the people's refusal to listen to the LORD?

options.text: Assyrian commanders captured him, put a hook in his nose, bound him in bronze shackles, and took him to Babylon.
options.note: Verse 11 records this specific, humiliating captivity.

options.text: He was allowed to continue reigning without any consequence.
options.note: The text explicitly describes severe consequence, capture and exile, following the unheeded warnings.

options.text: He was immediately executed by his own people.
options.note: He was captured and exiled by Assyria, not executed by his own people at this point; his eventual restoration follows this captivity.

options.text: No consequence for ignoring the LORD's warnings is described.
options.note: Verse 11 explicitly describes this severe consequence.

quiz.q: What did Manasseh do in the depths of his captivity that led to his restoration?

options.text: He sought the favor of the LORD his God and earnestly humbled himself before the God of his fathers.
options.note: Verse 12 records this genuine repentance that preceded his restoration.

options.text: He formed a military alliance with Babylon against Assyria.
options.note: The text describes spiritual repentance and prayer, not a political or military alliance, as leading to his restoration.

options.text: He continued in his idolatry without any change of heart.
options.note: The text explicitly describes a genuine change of heart and earnest humbling, not continued idolatry.

options.text: No response from Manasseh during his captivity is recorded.
options.note: Verse 12 explicitly records his repentance during captivity.

quiz.q: How did the LORD respond to Manasseh's repentance?

options.text: The LORD received his plea, heard his petition, and brought him back to Jerusalem and his kingdom.
options.note: Verse 13 records this full, gracious restoration in response to his repentance.

options.text: The LORD ignored his prayer and left him in captivity permanently.
options.note: The text explicitly describes the LORD hearing and restoring him, the opposite of ignoring his prayer.

options.text: The LORD forgave him personally but never restored his throne.
options.note: The text explicitly states he was returned to Jerusalem and to his kingdom, not merely granted personal forgiveness without restoration.

options.text: No divine response to Manasseh's repentance is recorded.
options.note: Verse 13 explicitly records the LORD's gracious response.

quiz.q: How did Amon's reign differ from his father Manasseh's, despite following the same pattern of idolatry?

options.text: Amon served the same idols his father had made but did not humble himself before the LORD as his father eventually did, and instead increased his guilt.
options.note: Verses 22 to 23 give this exact, explicit contrast between father and son.

options.text: Amon was far more righteous than his father had ever been.
options.note: The text explicitly states Amon did evil as his father had done and never humbled himself, the opposite of greater righteousness.

options.text: Amon repented even more thoroughly than his father Manasseh.
options.note: The text explicitly states Amon did not humble himself as his father had done, the opposite of a more thorough repentance.

options.text: No comparison between Amon and Manasseh's reigns is given.
options.note: Verses 22 to 23 explicitly compare their reigns and responses to the LORD.

questions: Manasseh's evil reached depths that seemed beyond any possible restoration, yet God fully received his repentance. Is there a sin in your own past, or a person you know, that you have quietly assumed is beyond the reach of God's mercy?

It took the extremity of captivity in Babylon before Manasseh genuinely humbled himself. What circumstance in your own life might God be using, even an uncomfortable one, to bring you to genuine humility before Him?

Even after Manasseh's repentance, some effects of his sin, the high places among the people, lingered on. Where do you see the lasting consequences of past choices, even ones you have genuinely repented of, still working themselves out in your life or family?

Amon watched his own father experience God's mercy after true repentance, yet refused to humble himself in the same way. Whose example of repentance and grace have you personally witnessed, and are you applying that same lesson to your own life?

Manasseh's story shows that God's grace can reach the worst offender the moment they turn to Him. Who in your life needs to hear this specific story, that no sin places them beyond hope of God's mercy?

translationNotes.note: A hook in his nose reflects a known Assyrian practice of leading captured or subjugated rulers by a ring or hook, a humiliating custom depicted in surviving Assyrian reliefs from this era.

The chronicler cites two distinct sources, the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel and the Records of the Seers, for Manasseh's prayer and repentance, material not preserved in the parallel account of 2 Kings 21, which records only his sins.

The note that the people still sacrificed at the high places, but only to the LORD, shows a partial but incomplete reform, real devotion redirected to the true God even while the improper location of worship persisted, a lingering effect not fully corrected until Josiah's later, more thorough reforms.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Chronicles 33:1-25. No em dashes or en dashes appeared in the source text for this chapter.
